title: Autonomous Blog Growth Engine
subtitle: AI-powered, multilingual SEO content pipeline built with n8n + OpenAI.
summary: Turned a multi-hour SEO content workflow into an automated decision engine — researcher → strategist → writer → translator → knowledge base.
problem: B2B SaaS content teams burn 2–4 hours per article on keyword research, briefs, writing, and localisation. Velocity stalls, SEO structure drifts, and cost per article climbs.
opportunity: Treat AI not as a writer, but as a multi-stage decision engine that mimics a growth team — so humans can focus on what content deserves to exist, not how to ship it.
approach: Built a six-stage n8n pipeline (Market Intelligence → Keyword Prioritisation → SEO Brief → AI Writing → Localisation → Knowledge Repository) wired to the OpenAI API and Google Workspace.
experiments: Weighted opportunity scoring across 30+ keywords (volume + competition + difficulty).
Brief generator producing title, meta, H1/H2/H3, semantic keywords, FAQs, CTA.
MENA-focused English → Arabic localisation that preserves SEO context.
Auto-publishing to Sheets + Docs as a reusable content knowledge base.
lessons: Most content velocity wins come from removing decisions, not writing faster.
AI workflows need taste — guardrails against keyword stuffing matter more than the model.

title: Monetising AI Latency
subtitle: The economics of conversational advertising during model inference.
summary: A consulting-style product case exploring whether AI response latency can be turned into contextual ad inventory — subsidising inference cost without breaking trust.
problem: LLM inference cost scales linearly with usage, while existing revenue models (subs, API pricing) cap revenue per query. Every prompt also burns unused user attention during generation.
opportunity: Convert AI latency into a new ad surface: Prompt → AI Processing → Contextual Micro-Experience → Response. Sponsored recos, brand cards, micro-video, contextual commerce.
approach: Evaluated through a four-lens framework — Market Analysis, Product Design, Business & Economics, Feasibility — landing on revenue per query, unit economics, and trust-preserving formats.
experiments: Sized attention inventory created by typical 2–6s response windows.
Modelled revenue per query and break-even against per-token inference cost.
Sketched ad formats ranked by perceived intrusion vs. contextual relevance.
lessons: Latency is a feature, not a bug — if the experience earns the wait.
Trust collapses faster than CTR rises; format choice beats placement choice.

title: Moving Targets
subtitle: India's first ride-hail advertising marketplace — turning fleets into measurable OOH.
summary: Designed a two-sided marketplace connecting brands directly to ride-hail drivers, with GPS-powered impression tracking and dynamic pricing.
problem: Vehicle advertising is fragmented and unmeasured. Brands juggle small agencies, drivers get one-off bonuses, and there's no ROI data on mobile OOH despite 1M+ cabs on Indian roads.
opportunity: A ₹18B serviceable market hiding in plain sight — if you can centralise supply, measure impressions with GPS, and automate permits and payouts.
approach: Built the product around four shifts: supply control, real-time measurement, recurring driver economics, and regulatory-by-default.
experiments: Smart fleet matching by route, location, and audience movement.
AI geofencing for context-aware campaigns in high-value zones.
Dynamic pricing engine adjusting for route quality, traffic, and peak hours.
Automated permit & RTO compliance per city.
lessons: Marketplaces win on measurement before they win on supply.
Driver economics is the real distribution channel — recurring beats lump-sum.

title: Pocket FM — Social Discovery
subtitle: Turning passive audio consumption into a viral, share-led growth engine.
summary: Designed share-led discovery for serialized audio: clip cards, audiograms, and group-listening rituals that move PFM from consumption to community.
problem: PFM users binge serialized stories for hours but the experience is private. No way to share a cliffhanger or recommend a moment — so organic acquisition and network effects stall.
opportunity: Stories are now the default sharing format. Combine serialized audio with native social — and audio becomes a Spotify-style growth engine, not a podcast app.
approach: Layered a social sharing ecosystem on top of existing playback: shareable clip cards, in-app audiograms, and gamified group-listening rituals.
experiments: One-tap 15s clip → dynamic story card with animated waveform → deep link to timestamp.
Playable audiograms doubling as external acquisition hook and internal retention loop.
Listening parties, quote challenges, and invite-based co-listening.
lessons: People share to express identity, not to broadcast content.
Internal feeds matter more than external shares for retention compounding.

title: Rapido: Radical Transparency
subtitle: Feature teardown — does showing live driver rejections actually improve UX?
summary: A teardown of Rapido's live 'X of Y captains rejected' UI — the trade-offs between transparency, control, and emotional friction in a two-sided marketplace.
problem: Ride-matching is a black box. Users wait in silence, unsure if the app is stuck, the fare is too low, or they should switch platforms. Cancellations rise with ambiguity.
opportunity: Expose the supply side. Stream live captain-rejection events to the rider so the invisible backend becomes a visible, actionable interaction.
approach: Mapped the new UX flow, listed the product hypotheses behind the choice, and pressure-tested them against community sentiment and behavioural design theory.
experiments: Hypothesis: Transparency reduces uncertainty even when the news is bad.
Hypothesis: Visible rejections nudge users to surge their bid or pickup.
Counter-hypothesis: Rejection counters create emotional friction that erodes trust.
lessons: Users don't want more information — they want information they can act on.
How and when you frame data matters more than whether you show it.
